title: Origami cicada
subtitle: Folding a traditional cicada from a single square
excerpt: The origami cicada is one of the oldest identifiable origami forms in the Japanese tradition, appearing in early pattern books and used in traditional ceremony. The model uses a small number of folds to produce a recognisable insect silhouette with folded wings and a pointed body. It is achievable at intermediate skill level from a 15 cm square.
sourceNotes: The cicada is documented in the Hiden Senbazuru Orikata (Secret Thousand Crane Folding, 1797, public domain via the National Diet Library of Japan), the earliest surviving printed origami compendium, which includes the cicada as one of its base models.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold in which the paper folds toward the viewer, creating a valley crease. Shown in origami diagrams as a dashed line with an arrow indicating the fold direction.
A fold in which the paper folds away from the viewer, creating a ridge or mountain crease. Shown in origami diagrams as a dot-dash line.
A fold in which the tip of a flap is pushed inward between the two layers of a point, reversing the direction of the crease. Used to form the head and body tip of the cicada.

to form its wings, with a single
at the tip to form the head. Use kami with the colour side facing down to begin.

items.name: Kami origami paper, 15 cm
items.notes: A single square, colour side down to begin. The cicada reads well in a plain colour or in a semi-translucent paper that suggests the texture of insect wings.

items.name: Mini bone folder (optional)
items.notes: For sharpening creases in the layered wing sections.

content.text: Fold the square in half diagonally both ways (colour side in), unfolding each time to create two diagonal creases. Fold in half horizontally and vertically (colour side out), unfolding each time. Collapse the square along the existing creases so that the two horizontal folds become mountain folds and the two diagonal folds become valley folds: the result is a flat triangle with an open edge at the bottom. This is the waterbomb base.

With the waterbomb base pointing downward (closed point at top, open flaps at bottom), fold the left bottom corner up to the centre top point and sharpen the crease. Repeat with the right bottom corner. The two folds create angled wing shapes at each side. Fold the top layer of the bottom point up and over toward the top point, exposing the white paper beneath.
Form the head with an inside reverse fold

Fold the top point down by approximately 10 mm to form the head of the cicada. Sharpen the crease. Perform the inside reverse fold: open the two layers of the folded point slightly, push the folded tip inward between the layers, and close the layers back together. The tip now points downward slightly between the two main body layers, forming a rounded head. Turn the model over: the colour side is now the back of the cicada, and the wing silhouette is visible from above.

items.symptom: The inside reverse fold tears the paper at the head
items.cause: The pre-crease for the reverse fold was not established before pushing inward.
items.fix: Fold the tip down and back again once before the reverse fold, establishing a sharp crease in both directions. The paper needs both the forward and backward crease to open easily for the reverse.

items.cause: The two wing folds were not made to the same angle.
items.fix: Fold both wings simultaneously if possible, or measure the fold angle against the centre line before creasing.
